learning_objectives: Define organizational behavior and its relevance to management
Identify the three levels of OB analysis: individual, group, organizational
Understand how OB research informs management practice

lecture_notes.introduction: Organizational behavior studies how people behave in organizations. Understanding OB is essential for anyone who works with and through other people.
key_concepts: OB definition: study of individual and group behavior in organizational settings
Three levels: individual (personality, motivation, perception), group (teams, conflict, leadership), organizational (culture, structure, change)
Evidence-based management: making decisions based on research, not intuition alone
Contributing disciplines: psychology, social psychology, sociology, anthropology
Systematic study vs. intuition: why common sense about people is often wrong
lecture_notes.demonstration: Debunking three common management myths using OB research evidence

review.value: Individual (why does this person behave this way?), Group (how does the team function?), Organizational (how does the culture shape behavior?)
Research shows open offices reduce communication, financial incentives can reduce motivation, and brainstorming produces fewer ideas than individual work

learning_objectives: Apply the Big Five personality model to workplace behavior
Understand how personality affects job performance and teamwork
Recognize the role of emotional intelligence in organizational effectiveness

lecture_notes.introduction: People are different. Understanding personality differences helps managers assign work effectively, build diverse teams, and reduce interpersonal conflict.
key_concepts: Big Five model: openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, neuroticism (OCEAN)
Conscientiousness: strongest predictor of job performance across most jobs
Myers-Briggs (MBTI): popular but limited predictive validity; useful for self-awareness, not hiring
Emotional intelligence: self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, social skills
Person-job fit and person-organization fit: matching personality to role and culture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Big Five self-assessment exercise and discussion of how traits affect team dynamics

review.value: Big Five has strong research support for predicting workplace behavior; MBTI is widely used but has weak predictive validity
Emotional intelligence accounts for up to 90% of the difference between average and outstanding leaders at senior levels

learning_objectives: Compare content theories: Maslow, Herzberg, McClelland
Apply process theories: expectancy, equity, goal-setting
Design motivation strategies for different workplace scenarios

lecture_notes.introduction: Motivation explains why people exert effort. Different theories illuminate different aspects of this complex phenomenon.
key_concepts: Content theories: WHAT motivates (needs, desires, goals)
Process theories: HOW motivation works (expectation, comparison, goal pursuit)
Expectancy theory: Motivation = Expectancy x Instrumentality x Valence
Equity theory: people compare their input-outcome ratio to others; inequity creates tension
Goal-setting theory: specific, challenging goals with feedback produce highest performance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a motivation strategy for a demoralized customer service team using multiple theories

review.value: Effort leads to performance (expectancy), performance leads to outcome (instrumentality), outcome is valued (valence); all three must be present
SMART goals + feedback + commitment = highest performance; the most practical and well-supported motivation theory

learning_objectives: Identify factors that determine team effectiveness
Apply strategies for managing groupthink and social loafing

lecture_notes.introduction: Teams are the basic work unit in modern organizations. Understanding what makes teams effective is a core management competency.
key_concepts: Group vs. team: groups share information; teams produce collective work products
Team effectiveness model: context (resources, leadership), composition (skills, diversity, size), process (norms, conflict, cohesion)
Groupthink: conformity pressure suppresses dissent and produces poor decisions
Social loafing: individuals exert less effort in groups; countered by accountability and task significance
Optimal team size: 5-9 members for most tasks; larger teams have diminishing returns
lecture_notes.demonstration: Team effectiveness diagnostic exercise applied to a real or simulated project team
setup_requirements: Groupthink case study (Bay of Pigs or Challenger)

review.value: Assign a devil's advocate, encourage dissent, bring in outside perspectives, use anonymous voting for decisions
Make individual contributions identifiable, set clear individual accountability, keep teams small, and make the work meaningful

learning_objectives: Identify sources of power in organizations
Navigate organizational politics ethically and effectively
Assess organizational culture and its impact on behavior
readings: Schein, Organizational Culture and Leadership, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Power and politics are realities of organizational life. Effective managers understand them and use them ethically to get things done.
key_concepts: Power bases: legitimate, reward, coercive, expert, referent (French & Raven)
Influence tactics: rational persuasion, inspirational appeal, consultation, exchange, coalitions
Organizational politics: activities to acquire, develop, and use power to obtain preferred outcomes
Organizational culture: shared values, beliefs, and assumptions that shape behavior
Culture assessment: artifacts, espoused values, basic underlying assumptions (Schein's levels)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing organizational culture using Schein's three levels for a company case study

review.value: Most effective for leadership; people follow because they respect your expertise or like you, not because they must
An organization's culture determines what behaviors are actually rewarded, regardless of what policies say

learning_objectives: Apply Lewin's and Kotter's change models to organizational change scenarios
Understand resistance to change from an OB perspective

lecture_notes.introduction: Organizations must change to survive. Understanding the human dynamics of change is essential for managing it successfully.
key_concepts: Lewin's model: unfreeze (create dissatisfaction with status quo), change (move to new state), refreeze (stabilize)
Kotter's 8 steps: urgency, coalition, vision, communication, empowerment, quick wins, consolidation, anchoring
Resistance sources: habit, security, economic factors, fear of unknown, selective information processing
Overcoming resistance: education, participation, facilitation, negotiation, manipulation (last resort), coercion (last resort)
Organizational development: planned change interventions to improve effectiveness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a change intervention for a department restructuring using Kotter's 8-step model

review.value: People who participate in designing the change are less likely to resist it; involvement creates ownership
Early visible successes within 6-18 months build momentum and credibility for the broader change effort

questions.question: Using expectancy theory, explain why a highly skilled employee might show low motivation. Identify which component (expectancy, instrumentality, or valence) is most likely the problem and propose a specific managerial intervention.
questions.answer: A highly skilled employee has high expectancy (they know their effort produces performance). Low motivation likely stems from low instrumentality (they don't believe performance leads to outcomes they value, perhaps because promotions go to those with connections rather than merit) or low valence (the available rewards, such as a small bonus, don't matter to them). Managerial intervention for low instrumentality: create a transparent performance-reward link by publishing promotion criteria, providing consistent performance-based recognition, and ensuring the employee sees clear examples of good performance being rewarded. For low valence: discover what the employee actually values (perhaps autonomy, challenging work, or flexibility rather than money) and adjust rewards accordingly.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the most likely component failure
Explains reasoning using expectancy theory correctly

questions.question: Compare the Big Five personality trait 'conscientiousness' with 'agreeableness' in terms of predicting job performance. Which is a better predictor overall, and in what roles might agreeableness be more important?
questions.answer: Conscientiousness is a better predictor of job performance overall because it reflects dependability, organization, self-discipline, and persistence, qualities that benefit virtually every job. Research consistently shows conscientiousness has the strongest correlation with job performance across occupations. Agreeableness (cooperativeness, trust, helpfulness) is a weaker predictor overall because some jobs benefit from assertiveness and tough-mindedness. However, agreeableness is more important in: (1) customer service roles where empathy and patience directly affect customer satisfaction, (2) collaborative team environments where harmony and cooperation are essential, (3) caregiving professions (nursing, social work) where compassion is a core competency, (4) conflict mediation roles where building trust is essential.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies conscientiousness as better overall predictor
Explains why with reference to research
Identifies specific roles where agreeableness matters more

questions.question: A project team of 12 consistently produces mediocre results despite having individually talented members. Using OB concepts, identify three possible causes and propose one intervention for each.
questions.answer: (1) Social loafing: with 12 members, individual contributions may be hard to identify, reducing effort. Intervention: break into sub-teams of 3-4 with clear individual deliverables and accountability. (2) Groupthink: if the team has high cohesion and a dominant leader, dissenting ideas may be suppressed, producing consensus but not quality. Intervention: assign a rotating devil's advocate role and use anonymous idea generation before group discussion. (3) Process loss: as team size increases, coordination overhead consumes productive time; meetings become inefficient. Intervention: reduce the core team to 7-8 members and have specialists participate only when their expertise is needed.

rubric.key: Interventions are specific and address the identified cause
Demonstrates understanding of group dynamics theory

questions.question: Explain the difference between Schein's three levels of organizational culture and provide an example where espoused values contradict basic underlying assumptions.
questions.answer: Schein's three levels: (1) Artifacts: visible structures and processes (office layout, dress code, meeting rituals), (2) Espoused values: stated strategies, goals, and philosophies (mission statements, value posters), (3) Basic underlying assumptions: unconscious, taken-for-granted beliefs that actually drive behavior. Example of contradiction: A company's espoused value is 'we encourage work-life balance' (posted on the website and in orientation). However, the basic underlying assumption is 'people who leave at 5 PM are not committed.' Evidence: promotions consistently go to people who work late, managers email on weekends expecting responses, and anyone using flexible work policies is passed over for key projects. The artifacts (emails at midnight, empty desks of promoted people at 5 PM) reveal the assumption that contradicts the espoused value.

rubric.key: Provides a convincing example of contradiction
Identifies observable artifacts that reveal the assumption

major_assignments.title: Organizational Behavior Analysis
major_assignments.description: Analyze an organization you have experience with (work, internship, volunteer) using OB frameworks. Assess individual motivation, team dynamics, power/politics, and culture, then propose evidence-based improvements.
objectives: Apply OB theories to analyze real organizational behavior
Identify root causes of behavioral problems using theoretical frameworks
Propose evidence-based interventions grounded in OB research
Demonstrate critical thinking about the gap between theory and practice
deliverables: Organizational behavior analysis paper (10-12 pages) covering: motivation analysis (applying 2+ theories), team dynamics assessment (using effectiveness model), power and politics analysis, culture assessment (using Schein's model), and evidence-based improvement recommendations (3-5 specific interventions)

criteria: Multiple OB theories correctly applied to the organizational context
Theories used as analytical tools, not just described
Analysis demonstrates understanding of theory limitations
Specific observable behaviors cited as evidence
Root cause analysis connects behaviors to OB concepts
Analysis distinguishes symptoms from underlying causes
Each recommendation grounded in OB research
Recommendations address root causes, not just symptoms
Proper citation of theories and research

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on OB theories, concepts, and applications

ob_analysis_paper.description: Organizational behavior analysis and recommendations paper

midterm_examination.description: Individual behavior, motivation, and personality theories

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all OB topics

class_participation.description: Active participation in case discussions, self-assessments, and group exercises

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Group exercises and case discussions require prepared participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Group exercises are collaborative. The OB analysis paper is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Properly cite all OB theories and research. Organizational examples should protect confidentiality of real individuals.
course_policies.technology_requirements: No specialized software required. Word processor for papers. Self-assessment tools provided in class.
